critique.summary: The article discusses the discovery of two new Azure Entra ID sign-in log bypass vulnerabilities, named GraphGoblin and Graph******, which allow attackers to obtain valid tokens without generating sign-in logs. These bypasses follow two previously disclosed vulnerabilities (GraphNinja and GraphGhost) and highlight ongoing issues with Microsoft's Azure sign-in logging security. The author also critiques Microsoft's handling of these vulnerabilities and provides detection methods for administrators.
mainPoints: Four Azure Entra ID sign-in log bypasses have been discovered since 2023, with the latest two (GraphGoblin and Graph******) allowing full token retrieval without logging.
GraphGoblin exploits repeated valid scope parameters to overflow logging database fields, causing sign-in logs to be omitted.
Graph****** uses an excessively long user-agent string to similarly bypass logging by overflowing database fields.
Microsoft fixed these vulnerabilities quickly after disclosure but classified the severity as 'Moderate' rather than 'Important', denying bounties or acknowledgments for the latest bypasses.
The author provides KQL queries to detect these bypasses by correlating Graph API activity logs with sign-in logs, requiring an Azure E5 license.
The article criticizes Microsoft’s inconsistent security review process and lack of public acknowledgment for these critical logging bypasses.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about the existence of multiple Azure Entra ID sign-in log bypass vulnerabilities are consistent with known security research practices and the author's detailed technical explanations, including demonstration commands and timelines. The vulnerabilities described align with typical authentication and logging issues such as parameter fuzzing and database field overflow. The author provides evidence including screenshots, video demos, and timelines of disclosure and fixes, which supports the credibility of the findings. Microsoft's classification of the severity and handling of these issues is also plausible given known variability in vendor responses to security reports. However, the article is a single-source blog post without independent third-party verification or official Microsoft statements, so while the technical details are plausible and well-documented by the author, external confirmation would strengthen the claims.
considerations: The article is authored by a security researcher on a personal or company blog, not a major journalistic outlet, so it lacks multiple independent sources or perspectives.
Microsoft's official response or detailed technical analysis of these vulnerabilities is not included, limiting the ability to fully verify the claims.
The impact of these bypasses depends on the presence and configuration of logging and monitoring solutions in affected organizations, which may vary widely.
The detection methods require an Azure E5 license, which may not be accessible to all organizations relying on Azure Entra ID logs.
The article does not discuss potential mitigations beyond detection or the broader implications for Azure security posture beyond logging.
The timeline and severity assessment reflect the author's perspective and may not represent Microsoft's internal risk evaluation or prioritization criteria.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around multiple Azure security and logging issues, highlighting real-world incidents of audit log inaccuracies, bypass vulnerabilities, and systemic complexity in Azure's identity and access management. Commenters share personal experiences and critiques of Microsoft's cloud services, emphasizing concerns about trustworthiness and transparency in audit logs and the challenges posed by Azure's architecture.

comments.author: deathanatos
comments.text:
IIRC, (& I don't remember if I reported it), but Azure's audit logs don't reflect reality when you delete a client secret from the UI, either.

If I remember the issue right, we lost a client secret (it just vanished!) and I went to the audit logs to see who dun it. According to the logs, I had done it. And yet, I also knew that I had not done it.

I eventually reconstructed the bug to an old page load. I had the page loaded when there were just secrets "A" & "B". When I then clicked the delete icon for "B", Azure deleted secrets "B" and "C" … which had been added since the page load. Essentially, the UI said "delete this row" but the API was "set the set of secrets to {A}". The audit log then logged the API "correctly" in the sense of, yes, my credentials did execute that API call, I suppose, but utterly incorrectly in the sense of any reasonable real-world view as to what I had done.

Thankfully we got it sorted, but it sort of shook my faith in Azure's logs in particular, and a little bit of audit logs in general. You have to make sure you've actually audited what the human did. Or, conversely, if you're trying to reason with audit logs, … you'd best understand how they were generated.

I don't think I would ever accept audit logs in court, if I were on a jury. Audit logs being hot lies is within reasonable doubt.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, plausible explanation of a specific audit log inconsistency based on personal experience, illustrating a real-world issue with Azure's logging fidelity.

comments.author: jiggawatts
comments.text:
Reminds me of an Azure Support ticket I submitted a few years ago when some developer clicked the "Fix this now" button in Application Insights, which then proceeded to double the scale of an already too-large App Service Plan. [1]

The Audit log showed the service identity of Application Insights, not the user that pressed the button! The cloud ops team changed the size back, and then the mysterious anonymous developer... changed it back. We had to have an "all hands" meeting to basically yell at the whole room to cut that out. Nobody fessed up, so we still don't know who it was.

The Azure Support tech argued with me vehemently that this was by design, that Azure purposefully obscures the identity of users in audit logs!!!

At first I was absolutely flabbergasted that anyone even remotely associated with a security audit log design could be this stupid, but then something clicked for me and it all started making sense:

    Entra Id logs are an evolution of Office 365 logs.
Microsoft developed Entra ID (original Azure Active Directory) initially for the sole purpose of Microsoft 365, not for the modern Azure Public Cloud!

They have a legitimate need to protect customer PII, hence the logs don't always contain their customers' private information. I.e.: Microsoft's subcontractors and outsourced support staff don't need and shouldn't see some of this information!

The problem was that they re-used the same code, the same architecture decisions, the same security tradeoffs for what are essentially 100% private systems. We need to see who on our payroll is monkeying around with our servers! There is NO expectation of privacy! GDPR does NOT apply! We're not even in Europe for crying out loud!

To this day I still see gaps in their logging where someone just "oops" forgot to log the identity of the account triggering the action. The most frustrating one for me is that Deployments don't log the user! It's one of only three administrative APIs that they have!

[1] As an aside: The plan had a 3-year Reservation on it, which meant that we were now paying for the original plan and something twice the size and non-Reserved! This was something like 5x the original cost, with no warning and no obvious way to see from the Portal UI that you're changing away from a Reserved size.
comments.analysis: The comment offers an insightful analysis of why Azure's audit logs lack user detail, linking it to legacy design choices and privacy considerations, which aligns with known challenges in cloud audit logging.

comments.author: kjellsbells
comments.text:
Puts me in mind of this scathing report from CISA on how a state-sponsored group broke into Microsoft and then into the State Department and a bunch of other agencies. Reads like a heist movie.

https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/2024-03/CSRB%20Revi...

What I found most incredible about the story is that it wasn't Microsoft who found the intrusion. It was some sysadmin at State who saw that some mail logs did not look right and investigated.
comments.analysis: This comment references a credible external CISA report and highlights the importance of vigilant internal monitoring, adding context to Azure security concerns.

comments.author: cyberax
comments.text:
Azure Entra is an example of making a system so complex that nobody can understand it entirely. I'm fairly experienced in access control systems, OIDC, crypto, etc. but I was not able to understand how it all fits together.

Google Cloud is simplistic in comparison. AWS is full of legacy complexity (IAM policies, sigh) but it's fairly self-contained and can be worked around by splitting stuff into accounts.

I have not looked at Oracle cloud yet. Is it any better than MS?
comments.analysis: The comment provides a reasoned comparison of cloud IAM systems, reflecting common industry perceptions about Azure's complexity versus competitors.

comments.author: ronbenton
comments.text: Bypassing logging feels relatively unimportant compared to some of the recent EntraID vulns we’ve seen
comments.analysis: This comment contextualizes the discussed bypasses as relatively minor compared to other known vulnerabilities, offering a prioritization perspective.

comments.author: epistasis
comments.text: There's a big tradeoff here though: IT admins really love buying Microsoft. And when the dog tries to complain about the dogfood, the dogfood purchaser tends to not understand very well.
comments.analysis: This is a general observation about customer behavior and product criticism, less technical but relevant to understanding market dynamics.

comments.author: dfedbeef
comments.text:
> It's not often that you see a demo of an actual Azure vulnerability, as they get patched and are gone forever. However, because Microsoft was having trouble replicating this complicated bypass, and asked for a video, I come bearing receipts.

Absolutely savage lol

[If you didn't read the thing, it's one curl command.]
comments.analysis: While brief and informal, the comment underscores the accessibility of the exploit, reinforcing the seriousness of the vulnerability.

comments.author: fuckinpuppers
comments.text: It is shocking how absolutely garbage azure is.
comments.analysis: This comment is an emotional outburst lacking detail or constructive content, reducing its analytical value.

comments.author: strbean
comments.text: Maybe I can use one of these to get in to my organization azure account from my alma mater. The email was deleted right after I graduated, but Microsoft has been trying to bill me (for a reserved IP or something) for close to a decade. Support is useless of course.
comments.analysis: This personal anecdote highlights billing and support frustrations but does not contribute technical insight into the discussed vulnerabilities.

comments.author: throwoutway
comments.text:
Yesterday ProPublica and ArsTechnica published a takedown of Azure: "Federal cyber experts called Microsoft’s cloud a “pile of shit,” approved it anyway" ...

https://arstechnica.com/information-technology/2026/03/feder...
comments.analysis: This comment references external media criticism, adding context but without technical detail or personal experience.
